preamble.summary: Proverbs 6 is the most topically diverse chapter in the book's first nine chapters, addressing four distinct subjects: the danger of guaranteeing a neighbor's debt (vv1-5), the lesson of the ant for the sluggard (vv6-11), the profile of the worthless troublemaker (vv12-15), the seven things the LORD detests (vv16-19), and a renewed warning against adultery (vv20-35). The chapter reads like a wisdom handbook, moving from financial prudence to work ethic to character assessment to moral absolutes to sexual fidelity.
preamble.remarkable: The numerical proverb in verses 16-19 — 'six things the LORD hates, seven are detestable to Him' — is among the most theologically compressed passages in the Hebrew Bible. The ascending number pattern (six... seven) is a common ancient Near Eastern rhetorical device that signals the final item as the climax. The list moves from body parts to social effects: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that rush to evil, a false witness, and the one who sows discord among brothers. The climax is not violence or lying but the fracturing of community. For Proverbs, nothing is worse than destroying the unity of brothers. The ant passage (vv6-11) is notable for its implicit theology: the ant needs no commander, overseer, or ruler — it acts from internal wisdom. The sluggard, by contrast, has been given instruction and still refuses to act.
preamble.friction: The surety passage (vv1-5) reflects an economic context where personal guarantees could result in debt slavery. The advice to extract yourself immediately — 'like a gazelle from a hunter's hand' — may seem harsh toward the neighbor whose debt you guaranteed, but in the ancient world, financial overcommitment could destroy an entire household. The adultery passage (vv25-35) focuses almost entirely on the consequences for the male adulterer, including the jealous husband's rage. The woman is described primarily as a temptation, and the offended husband's fury is presented without critique. The text assumes a patriarchal legal framework in which adultery is primarily an offense against another man's household.
preamble.connections: The 'seven detestable things' list connects to the broader biblical pattern of numbered proverbs (Proverbs 30:15-31, Amos 1-2). The ant imagery has parallels in Egyptian wisdom literature. The adultery warning (vv20-35) extends the theme from chapter 5 and sets up the dramatic narrative of chapter 7. The body-parts progression in verses 16-19 (eyes, tongue, hands, heart, feet) connects to the body-parts instruction in 4:23-27 (heart, mouth, eyes, feet).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭נִי אִם־עָרַ֣בְתָּ לְרֵעֶ֑ךָ תָּקַ֖עְתָּ לַזָּ֣ר כַּפֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My son, if thou be surety for thy friend, if thou hast stricken thy hand with a stranger,
verses.rendering:
My son, if you have put up security for your neighbor,
if you have pledged yourself to a stranger with a handshake,
translator_notes: Aravta ('you have become surety, you have pledged') describes the act of guaranteeing another person's debt. Taqa'ta kappekha ('you have struck your hands') is the ancient equivalent of a handshake or contract — the physical gesture that seals a financial commitment. The urgency of what follows suggests that this pledge was impulsive and dangerous.

verses.text_hebrew: נוֹקַ֥שְׁתָּ בְאִמְרֵי־פִ֑יךָ נִ֝לְכַּ֗דְתָּ בְּאִמְרֵי־פִֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art snared with the words of thy mouth, thou art taken with the words of thy mouth.
verses.rendering:
you have been snared by the words of your mouth,
caught by the words of your mouth.
translator_notes: Noqashta ('you have been ensnared') and nilkadta ('you have been caught, captured') describe the pledge-maker as a trapped animal. The trap is imre fikha ('the words of your mouth') — your own speech has bound you. The repetition of the phrase emphasizes that the son's own words are the cage.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲשֵׂ֨ה זֹ֥את אֵפ֪וֹא ׀ בְּנִ֡י וְֽהִנָּצֵ֗ל כִּ֘י בָ֤אתָ בְכַף־רֵעֶ֑ךָ לֵ֥ךְ הִ֝תְרַפֵּ֗ס וּרְהַ֥ב רֵעֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Do this now, my son, and deliver thyself, when thou art come into the hand of thy friend; go, humble thyself, and make sure thy friend.
translator_notes: The escape plan requires two actions: hitrappes ('humble yourself, prostrate yourself, trample on your own pride') and rehav ('press urgently, storm, importune'). The first demands the surrender of dignity; the second demands aggressive persistence. Both are preferable to remaining trapped in a bad financial commitment.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תִּתֵּ֣ן שֵׁנָ֣ה לְעֵינֶ֑יךָ וּ֝תְנוּמָ֗ה לְעַפְעַפֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Give not sleep to thine eyes, nor slumber to thine eyelids.
verses.rendering:
Do not give sleep to your eyes
or slumber to your eyelids.
translator_notes: The urgency forbids rest: no shenah ('sleep') and no tenumah ('slumber, dozing'). The financial trap must be resolved immediately — every hour of delay increases the danger.

verses.text_hebrew: הִ֭נָּצֵל כִּצְבִ֣י מִיָּ֑ד וּ֝כְצִפּ֗וֹר מִיַּ֥ד יָקֽוּשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver thyself as a roe from the hand of the hunter, and as a bird from the hand of the fowler.
verses.rendering:
Free yourself like a gazelle from a hunter's hand,
like a bird from the fowler's grip.
translator_notes: The escape metaphors — tsevi ('gazelle') and tsippor ('bird') — emphasize speed and desperation. Both animals survive only by immediate, total flight. The yaqush ('fowler, bird-catcher') is the one who sets traps; the surety-giver has walked into one and must escape with the same urgency as prey fleeing a predator.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵֽךְ־אֶל־נְמָלָ֥ה עָצֵ֑ל רְאֵ֖ה דְרָכֶ֣יהָ וַחֲכָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Go to the ant, thou sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise:
verses.rendering:
Go to the ant, you sluggard;
observe her ways and become wise.
verses.expanded_rendering: The father redirects the son's attention from human instruction to creation itself. The nemalah ('ant') is held up as the teacher, and the atsel ('sluggard, lazy person') is the student. The command re'eh derakheiha ('observe her ways') uses the same language applied to observing wisdom's paths — the ant is a living mashal, a walking proverb. The instruction vachakham ('and become wise') implies that wisdom is available in nature for anyone willing to observe. The ant does not speak, does not write, does not hold lectures — it simply works, and its work is its sermon.
translator_notes: The atsel ('sluggard') is a distinctive Proverbs character who appears repeatedly (6:6, 9; 10:26; 13:4; 15:19; 19:24; 20:4; 21:25; 22:13; 24:30-34; 26:13-16). The sluggard is not merely tired but characterized by a particular kind of self-deception: always having a reason not to act.

key_terms.semantic_range: sluggard, lazy person, one who is slack, idle; someone who avoids effort through excuse-making
key_terms.note: The atsel in Proverbs is a comic and tragic figure — comic because his excuses are absurd ('there is a lion in the road!' 22:13), tragic because his inaction destroys him. The sluggard's problem is not inability but unwillingness.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֖ר אֵֽין־לָ֥הּ קָצִ֗ין שֹׁטֵ֥ר וּמֹשֵֽׁל׃
verses.text_kjv: Which having no guide, overseer, or ruler,
verses.rendering:
She has no commander,
no overseer, and no ruler,
translator_notes: Three terms of authority — qatsin ('commander, chief'), shoter ('overseer, officer'), and moshel ('ruler') — are absent from the ant's life. She needs no external management. The implied rebuke is sharp: the sluggard has father, teacher, and wisdom tradition — far more guidance than the ant — and still does less.

verses.text_hebrew: תָּכִ֣ין בַּקַּ֣יִץ לַחְמָ֑הּ אָגְרָ֥ה בַ֝קָּצִ֗יר מַאֲכָלָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Provideth her meat in the summer, and gathereth her food in the harvest.
verses.rendering:
yet she prepares her food in summer
and gathers her provisions at harvest.
translator_notes: The ant's two actions — takhin ('she prepares, she establishes') in summer and agrah ('she gathers, she stores') at harvest — demonstrate foresight. She works when conditions allow in order to survive when they do not. The sluggard fails precisely at this point: he cannot connect present effort to future need.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־מָתַ֖י עָצֵ֥ל ׀ תִּשְׁכָּ֑ב מָ֝תַ֗י תָּק֥וּם מִשְּׁנָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: How long wilt thou sleep, O sluggard? when wilt thou arise out of thy sleep?
verses.rendering:
How long will you lie there, sluggard?
When will you rise from your sleep?
translator_notes: The ad matay ('how long?') directed at the sluggard echoes Wisdom's own ad matay in 1:22. The question implies that the sluggard's sleep is not rest but evasion — he lies in bed to avoid the demands of the day.

verses.text_hebrew: מְעַ֣ט שֵׁ֭נוֹת מְעַ֣ט תְּנוּמ֑וֹת מְעַ֓ט ׀ חִבֻּ֖ק יָדַ֣יִם לִשְׁכָּֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Yet a little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep:
verses.rendering:
'A little sleep, a little slumber,
a little folding of the hands to rest' —
translator_notes: The sluggard's own voice appears in quotation: me'at shenot ('a little sleep'), me'at tenumot ('a little slumber'), me'at chibbuq yadayim ('a little folding of the hands'). The triple me'at ('a little, just a bit') is the sluggard's self-deception — each delay is small, but they accumulate into destruction. The charm of the passage is that the sluggard never asks for much; he just asks for it perpetually.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבָ֣א כִמְהַלֵּ֣ךְ רֵישֶׁ֑ךָ וּ֝מַחְסֹרְךָ֗ כְּאִ֣ישׁ מָגֵֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: So shall thy poverty come as one that travelleth, and thy want as an armed man.
verses.rendering:
and poverty will come upon you like a prowler,
and your need like an armed man.
translator_notes: Poverty (reshekha, 'your poverty') arrives kimhalekh ('like a traveler, like a prowler') — it approaches gradually, on foot, then suddenly it is at the door. Need (machsorekha, 'your lack') arrives ke-ish magen ('like a man with a shield, like an armed man') — by then it is a warrior you cannot fight off. The progression from quiet approach to armed assault captures how poverty works: it creeps before it conquers.

verses.text_hebrew: אָדָ֣ם בְּ֭לִיַּעַל אִ֣ישׁ אָ֑וֶן הוֹלֵ֖ךְ עִקְּשׁ֣וּת פֶּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A naughty person, a wicked man, walketh with a froward mouth.
verses.rendering:
A worthless person, a wicked man,
goes about with crooked speech.
translator_notes: Adam beliyya'al ('a person of worthlessness, a person of Belial') and ish aven ('a man of iniquity, a man of trouble'). Beliyya'al may derive from beli ('without') and ya'al ('profit, worth') — a person of no value. The troublemaker's primary tool is iqqeshut peh ('crookedness of mouth') — perverted, twisted speech.

verses.text_hebrew: קֹרֵ֣ץ בְּ֭עֵינָיו מֹלֵ֣ל בְּרַגְלָ֑יו מֹ֝רֶ֗ה בְּאֶצְבְּעֹתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He winketh with his eyes, he speaketh with his feet, he teacheth with his fingers;
verses.rendering:
He winks with his eyes,
signals with his feet,
points with his fingers.
translator_notes: Three body parts used for covert communication: eyes that qorets ('wink, pinch shut'), feet that molel ('scrape, shuffle, signal'), and fingers that moreh ('point, teach, direct'). The troublemaker communicates through a private language of gestures — he conspires in plain sight using signals his targets cannot read.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּהְפֻּכ֣וֹת בְּ֭לִבּוֹ חֹרֵ֣שׁ רָ֑ע בְּכָל־עֵ֝֗ת מִדְיָנִ֥ים יְשַׁלֵּֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Frowardness is in his heart, he deviseth mischief continually; he soweth discord.
translator_notes: Tahpukhot ('perversities, things turned upside down') are in his lev ('heart') — the command center is corrupted. He choresh ra ('plows evil, devises harm') at all times and yeshalle'ach midyanim ('sends out, dispatches strife'). The verb shalach ('send') with midyanim ('conflicts, disputes') presents discord as something deliberately launched, not accidentally caused.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־כֵּ֗ן פִּ֭תְאֹם יָב֣וֹא אֵיד֑וֹ פֶּ֥תַע יִ֝שָּׁבֵ֗ר וְאֵ֣ין מַרְפֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore shall his calamity come suddenly; suddenly shall he be broken without remedy.
verses.rendering:
Therefore disaster will come on him suddenly;
in an instant he will be shattered beyond healing.
translator_notes: Pit'om ('suddenly') and peta ('in an instant') — the same suddenness the troublemaker used against others now strikes him. His eido ('his disaster, his calamity') arrives without warning, and he yisshaver ('will be shattered, broken') ve-ein marpe ('and there is no healing, no remedy'). The sowing of discord produces a harvest of irreversible destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: שֶׁשׁ־הֵ֭נָּה שָׂנֵ֣א יְהוָ֑ה וְ֝שֶׁ֗בַע תּוֹעֲבַ֥ת נַפְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: These six things doth the LORD hate: yea, seven are an abomination unto him:
verses.rendering:
Six things the LORD hates;
seven are detestable to Him:
verses.expanded_rendering: The numerical proverb form — shesh... ve-sheva ('six... and seven') — is a characteristic device of ancient Near Eastern wisdom poetry (cf. Proverbs 30:15-31; Amos 1:3-2:6). The ascending number signals that the list is not exhaustive and that the final item is the climax. The LORD sane ('hates') and finds to'avat nafsho ('detestable to His soul, abominable to His very being'). The word to'evah ('abomination, detestable thing') is the strongest term of divine moral revulsion in Hebrew — the same word used for idolatry and the worst covenant violations. What follows is not a list of preferences but a catalogue of what God fundamentally opposes in the moral order.
translator_notes: The ascending numerical pattern (x... x+1) is found throughout the ancient Near East, from Ugaritic poetry to Amos's oracles against the nations. It creates anticipation: the hearer knows the final item will be the most important.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵינַ֣יִם רָ֭מוֹת לְשׁ֣וֹן שָׁ֑קֶר וְ֝יָדַ֗יִם שֹׁפְכ֥וֹת דָּם־נָקִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: A proud look, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood,
verses.rendering:
haughty eyes, a lying tongue,
and hands that shed innocent blood,
translator_notes: The list begins with three body parts: eynayim ramot ('eyes that are high, haughty, arrogant'), leshon shaqer ('a tongue of falsehood'), and yadayim shofkhot dam naqi ('hands pouring out innocent blood'). Eyes, tongue, hands — perception, speech, action, each corrupted. The dam naqi ('innocent blood') echoes the gratuitous violence of 1:11.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֗ב חֹרֵ֥שׁ מַחְשְׁב֥וֹת אָ֑וֶן רַגְלַ֥יִם מְ֝מַהֲר֗וֹת לָר֥וּץ לָרָעָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: An heart that deviseth wicked imaginations, feet that be swift in running to mischief,
verses.rendering:
a heart that devises wicked schemes,
feet that rush to run toward evil,
translator_notes: The list continues with heart (lev choresh machshevot aven, 'a heart plowing wicked plans') and feet (raglayim memaharot laruts lara'ah, 'feet hastening to run to evil'). The inward source (heart devising) produces the outward result (feet running). The verb charah ('to plow, to devise') applied to the heart shows that evil is cultivated, not accidental.

verses.text_hebrew: יָפִ֣יחַ כְּ֭זָבִים עֵ֣ד שָׁ֑קֶר וּמְשַׁלֵּ֥חַ מְ֝דָנִ֗ים בֵּ֣ין אַחִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: A false witness that speaketh lies, and he that soweth discord among brethren.
verses.rendering:
a false witness who breathes out lies,
and one who sows discord among brothers.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final two items — the climax. Ed shaqer ('a false witness') who yafiach kezavim ('breathes out lies, exhales falsehood') describes someone for whom lying is as natural as breathing; deception is not an occasional tactic but a respiratory function. Then the seventh and most detestable: umeshalle'ach medanim ben achim ('one who sends out, dispatches discord among brothers'). The word achim ('brothers') is the key. It does not merely mean siblings but fellow members of the community, covenant partners, those bound together by shared identity. To fracture this bond — to turn brother against brother — is, in God's assessment, worse than violence, worse than lying, worse than arrogance. It destroys the very fabric of community that God designed humans to inhabit. The climax of the list reveals what matters most to God: not individual virtue alone but communal unity. The supreme abomination is not a personal vice but a relational destruction.
translator_notes: The verb yafiach ('breathes out, blows') applied to lies makes falsehood involuntary and constant — the false witness does not decide to lie; he exhales lies. The placement of the discord-sower at the climax of a list that includes murder reveals Proverbs' communal priorities: fracturing brotherhood is worse than shedding blood.

verses.text_hebrew: נְצֹ֣ר בְּ֭נִי מִצְוַ֣ת אָבִ֑יךָ וְאַל־תִּ֝טֹּ֗שׁ תּוֹרַ֥ת אִמֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My son, keep thy father's commandment, and forsake not the law of thy mother:
verses.rendering:
Guard, my son, your father's command,
and do not forsake your mother's instruction.
translator_notes: The instruction from 1:8 returns nearly verbatim, reopening the parental instruction format for the adultery warning. The mother's torah stands in equal authority with the father's mitsvah.

verses.text_hebrew: קָשְׁרֵ֣ם עַל־לִבְּךָ֣ תָמִ֑יד עָ֝נְדֵ֗ם עַל־גַּרְגְּרוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Bind them continually upon thine heart, and tie them about thy neck.
verses.rendering:
Bind them on your heart continually;
tie them around your neck.
translator_notes: The binding imagery from 3:3 continues the Deuteronomic echo (Deuteronomy 6:8). The instructions must be physically attached — tamid ('continually, always') — to the heart and neck. The repetition of this imagery throughout the opening chapters creates a cumulative insistence: wear wisdom like clothing.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּהִתְהַלֶּכְךָ֨ ׀ תַּנְחֶ֬ה אֹתָ֗ךְ בְּ֭שָׁכְבְּךָ תִּשְׁמֹ֣ר עָלֶ֑יךָ וַ֝הֲקִיצ֗וֹתָ הִ֣יא תְשִׂיחֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: When thou goest, it shall lead thee; when thou sleepest, it shall keep thee; and when thou awakest, it shall talk with thee.
translator_notes: Three life-moments — walking, sleeping, waking — are all covered by parental instruction personified. It tancheh ('will lead, will guide'), tishmor ('will guard, will watch over'), and tesichekha ('will speak with you, will converse with you'). The instruction becomes a companion that is never absent — a guide on the road, a guardian in sleep, a conversation partner at dawn.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י נֵ֣ר מִ֭צְוָה וְת֣וֹרָה א֑וֹר וְדֶ֥רֶךְ חַ֝יִּ֗ים תּוֹכְח֥וֹת מוּסָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: For the commandment is a lamp; and the law is light; and reproofs of instruction are the way of life:
verses.rendering:
For the command is a lamp and instruction is a light,
and the reproofs of discipline are the way of life —
translator_notes: Mitsvah as ner ('lamp') and torah as or ('light') connect to the dawn imagery of 4:18. Instruction illuminates what darkness hides. The tokhechot musar ('reproofs of discipline') — the corrections that hurt — are derekh chayyim ('the way of life'). The road to life runs through correction, not around it.

verses.text_hebrew: לִ֭שְׁמָרְךָ מֵאֵ֣שֶׁת רָ֑ע מֵֽ֝חֶלְקַ֗ת לְשׁ֣וֹן נָכְרִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: To keep thee from the evil woman, from the flattery of the tongue of a strange woman.
verses.rendering:
to guard you from the evil woman,
from the smooth tongue of the outsider.
translator_notes: The lamp/light of instruction (v23) serves a specific protective function: guarding from the eshet ra ('evil woman, woman of harm') and from chelqat leshon nokhriiyyah ('the smoothness of the tongue of the foreign woman'). The same chelqah ('smoothness') from 5:3 returns — her weapon is always her speech.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּחְמֹ֣ד יָ֭פְיָהּ בִּלְבָבֶ֑ךָ וְאַל־תִּ֝קָּחֲךָ֗ בְּעַפְעַפֶּֽיהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Lust not after her beauty in thine heart; neither let her take thee with her eyelids.
verses.rendering:
Do not desire her beauty in your heart,
and do not let her capture you with her eyelids.
translator_notes: The verb tachmod ('desire, covet') is the same verb from the tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17). The adultery warning is grounded in the Decalogue — to desire the forbidden woman is to covet. Her af'appeiha ('her eyelids,' poetic for her glances) are the instruments of capture. Desire begins in the heart (bilevavekha) before it reaches the body.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י בְעַד־אִשָּׁ֥ה זוֹנָ֗ה עַֽד־כִּכַּ֫ר לָ֥חֶם וְאֵ֥שֶׁת אִ֑ישׁ נֶ֖פֶשׁ יְקָרָ֣ה תָצֽוּד׃
verses.text_kjv: For by means of a whorish woman a man is brought to a piece of bread: and the adulteress will hunt for the precious life.
verses.rendering:
For a prostitute reduces a man to a loaf of bread,
but a married woman hunts for a precious life.
translator_notes: Two categories are distinguished: the ishah zonah ('prostitute') costs you economically — she reduces a man ad kikkar lachem ('to a round of bread,' that is, to poverty). But the eshet ish ('wife of a man, married woman') does something worse: she tatsud ('hunts, stalks') a nefesh yeqarah ('a precious life, a valuable soul'). The prostitute takes your money; the adulteress takes your life.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲיַחְתֶּ֣ה אִ֭ישׁ אֵ֣שׁ בְּחֵיק֑וֹ וּ֝בְגָדָ֗יו לֹ֣א תִשָּׂרַֽפְנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Can a man take fire in his bosom, and his clothes not be burned?
verses.rendering:
Can a man carry fire against his chest
without his clothes being burned?
translator_notes: The first of two rhetorical questions with obvious answers. Fire (esh) against the cheq ('chest, bosom, lap') will burn the begadav ('his garments'). The question expects no answer because the answer is self-evident. Adultery is like carrying fire — the burning is not a possibility but a certainty.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־יְהַלֵּ֣ךְ אִ֭ישׁ עַל־הַגֶּחָלִ֑ים וְ֝רַגְלָ֗יו לֹ֣א תִכָּוֶֽינָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Can one go upon hot coals, and his feet not be burned?
verses.rendering:
Can a man walk on hot coals
without scorching his feet?
translator_notes: The second question intensifies: gechalim ('hot coals, embers') under the raglav ('his feet'). The answer, again, is obvious. The fire metaphor makes adultery a question of physics, not merely morality — consequences follow as inevitably as burns follow fire.

verses.text_hebrew: כֵּ֗ן הַ֭בָּא אֶל־אֵ֣שֶׁת רֵעֵ֑הוּ לֹ֥א יִ֝נָּקֶ֗ה כָּל־הַנֹּגֵ֥עַ בָּֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: So he that goeth in to his neighbour's wife; whosoever toucheth her shall not be innocent.
verses.rendering:
So it is with the man who goes to his neighbor's wife;
no one who touches her will go unpunished.
translator_notes: The application is direct: haba el eshet re'ehu ('the one who goes to his neighbor's wife') will lo yinnaqeh ('not be held innocent, not go unpunished'). The verb naqah ('to be clean, to be innocent, to be acquitted') in the negative means there is no escape from consequences. Kol hanoge'a bah ('everyone who touches her') extends the warning to any degree of involvement.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יָב֣וּזוּ לַ֭גַּנָּב כִּ֣י יִגְנ֑וֹב לְמַלֵּ֥א נַ֝פְשׁ֗וֹ כִּ֣י יִרְעָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Men do not despise a thief, if he steal to satisfy his soul when he is hungry;
verses.rendering:
People do not despise a thief
if he steals to fill his stomach when he is hungry,
translator_notes: A surprising comparison begins: theft from hunger receives understanding, not contempt. The lo yavuzu ('they do not despise') shows that the community recognizes the mitigating force of genuine need.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭נִמְצָא יְשַׁלֵּ֣ם שִׁבְעָתָ֑יִם אֶת־כָּל־ה֖וֹן בֵּית֣וֹ יִתֵּֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: But if he be found, he shall restore sevenfold; he shall give all the substance of his house.
verses.rendering:
but if caught, he must repay sevenfold;
he must hand over all the wealth of his house.
translator_notes: Even the understood thief faces consequences: shiv'atayim ('sevenfold') restitution, potentially kol hon beito ('all the wealth of his house'). The sevenfold payment exceeds the Mosaic requirement (Exodus 22:1-4 specifies four- or fivefold) and may represent a general idiom for complete restitution.

verses.text_hebrew: נֹאֵ֣ף אִ֭שָּׁה חֲסַר־לֵ֑ב מַ֝שְׁחִ֗ית נַפְשׁ֥וֹ ה֣וּא יַעֲשֶֽׂנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: But whoso committeth adultery with a woman lacketh understanding: he that doeth it destroyeth his own soul.
verses.rendering:
But the one who commits adultery lacks sense;
whoever does it destroys himself.
translator_notes: The comparison's point: theft from hunger is understandable but still costly. Adultery is not even understandable — it is chasar lev ('lacking heart, lacking sense'). There is no mitigating circumstance. The no'ef ('adulterer') is mashchit nafsho ('destroying his own soul, ruining himself'). The destruction is self-inflicted.

verses.text_hebrew: נֶֽגַע־וְקָל֥וֹן יִמְצָ֑א וְ֝חֶרְפָּת֗וֹ לֹ֣א תִמָּחֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A wound and dishonour shall he get; and his reproach shall not be wiped away.
verses.rendering:
He will find wounds and disgrace,
and his shame will not be wiped away.
translator_notes: Nega ('wound, blow, plague') and qalon ('disgrace, shame') — physical and social consequences. The cherpato ('his reproach, his disgrace') lo timmacheh ('will not be blotted out, erased'). Unlike the thief who can repay and restore his standing, the adulterer's reputation is permanently damaged.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־קִנְאָ֥ה חֲמַת־גָּ֑בֶר וְלֹֽא־יַ֝חְמ֗וֹל בְּי֣וֹם נָקָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For jealousy is the rage of a man: therefore he will not spare in the day of vengeance.
verses.rendering:
For jealousy is a husband's fury,
and he will show no mercy on the day of revenge.
translator_notes: Qin'ah ('jealousy, zeal, passionate anger') is chamat gaver ('the rage of a man, a husband's fury'). The gaver is the offended husband whose wife has been taken. His jealousy will not yachmol ('show mercy, spare, have compassion') on the yom naqam ('day of vengeance, day of retribution'). The social consequences of adultery include facing the wrath of a man with every reason to destroy you.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יִ֭שָּׂא פְּנֵ֣י כָל־כֹּ֑פֶר וְלֹֽא־יֹ֝אבֶ֗ה כִּ֣י תַרְבֶּה־שֹֽׁחַד׃
verses.text_kjv: He will not regard any ransom; neither will he rest content, though thou givest many gifts.
verses.rendering:
He will not accept any compensation;
he will not be willing, no matter how large the bribe.
translator_notes: Unlike the thief's debt, which can be repaid (v31), the adulterer's offense is beyond financial resolution. Lo yissa pene khol kofer ('he will not lift the face of any ransom') — no payment will satisfy the husband. Lo yoveh ki tarbeh shochad ('he will not consent though you multiply the bribe'). Money cannot purchase forgiveness for this offense. The adultery sequence ends where it began: with consequences that are as certain as fire and as inescapable as debt.
